text: Cryogenic extraction of noble gases from air and nuclear fuel mixtures is highly energy intensive. Hence, there is an urgent need for solid-state adsorbents that can separate the noble gases at ambient conditions. Although porous materials such as zeolites and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown great promise, detailed studies on the interaction of gases with these materials are scarce. In this context, we report on a new microporous calcium-based MOF denoted as SBMOF-2 that shows enhanced affinity towards Xe as compared to Kr. At ambient temperature, Xe/Kr selectivity is observed to be around 10 and Xe adsorption capacity is found to be 27.07 wt%. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction studies reveal that the cages present in the pores of the SBMOF-2 contain phenyl rings and -OH groups, which facilitate the strong adsorption of Xe atoms on account of their high polarisibility. Breakthrough curves of SBMOF-2 obtained from computational as well as experimental studies reveal the early elution of Kr in comparison with Xe, which affirms the application of SBMOF-2 in nuclear fuel reprocessing as well as air separation. Comparison of Xe capacity and Xe/Kr selectivity of SBMOF-2 with unmodified MOFs reveals that the performance of SBMOF-2 is competitive with that of the highest Xe capacity and selectivity values reported so far, which is observed for NiMOF-74.
